fr:mainmatter.fr:p: We consider the finitely presentable V-algebra V{\mathopen {}\left [\mathsf {x}\right ]\mathclose {}}, i.e. the free extension of V by an element \mathsf {x}. By synthetic quasicoherence, the canonical homomorphism V{\mathopen {}\left [\mathsf {x}\right ]\mathclose {}}\to V^{\operatorname {Spec}{V{\mathopen {}\left [\mathsf {x}\right ]\mathclose {}}}} is an isomorphism. We will see that this homomorphism is, in fact, tracked (up to isomorphism) by the desired map V+1\to V^V.

First of all, the carrier of V{\mathopen {}\left [\mathsf {x}\right ]\mathclose {}} is the coproduct V+1. The spectrum of V{\mathopen {}\left [\mathsf {x}\right ]\mathclose {}} can be seen to be V itself by the universal property of the free extension:

The canonical isomorphism above sends a homomorphism {\operatorname {Spec}{V{\mathopen {}\left [\mathsf {x}\right ]\mathclose {}}}}\xrightarrow {{f}}{V} to the element f\mathsf {x}. We therefore have the desired isomorphism V+1 \cong V{\mathopen {}\left [\mathsf {x}\right ]\mathclose {}} \cong V^{\operatorname {Spec}{V{\mathopen {}\left [\mathsf {x}\right ]\mathclose {}}}}\cong V^V.

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Synthetic quasicoherence in the object classifier

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let V be the generic object in the object classifier \mathbf {Set}{\mathopen {}\left [V\right ]\mathclose {}}. Then the map V+1\to V^V mapping \mathsf {inl}\,x to \lambda \_.x and \mathsf {inr}\,{\mathopen {}\left (\right )\mathclose {}} to \lambda x.x is an isomorphism.

We consider the finitely presentable V-algebra V{\mathopen {}\left [\mathsf {x}\right ]\mathclose {}}, i.e. the free extension of V by an element \mathsf {x}. By synthetic quasicoherence, the canonical homomorphism V{\mathopen {}\left [\mathsf {x}\right ]\mathclose {}}\to V^{\operatorname {Spec}{V{\mathopen {}\left [\mathsf {x}\right ]\mathclose {}}}} is an isomorphism. We will see that this homomorphism is, in fact, tracked (up to isomorphism) by the desired map V+1\to V^V.

First of all, the carrier of V{\mathopen {}\left [\mathsf {x}\right ]\mathclose {}} is the coproduct V+1. The spectrum of V{\mathopen {}\left [\mathsf {x}\right ]\mathclose {}} can be seen to be V itself by the universal property of the free extension:

The canonical isomorphism above sends a homomorphism {\operatorname {Spec}{V{\mathopen {}\left [\mathsf {x}\right ]\mathclose {}}}}\xrightarrow {{f}}{V} to the element f\mathsf {x}. We therefore have the desired isomorphism V+1 \cong V{\mathopen {}\left [\mathsf {x}\right ]\mathclose {}} \cong V^{\operatorname {Spec}{V{\mathopen {}\left [\mathsf {x}\right ]\mathclose {}}}}\cong V^V.

Let \mathbb {T} be a geometric theory and let U_{\mathbb {T}} be its generic model in \mathbf {Set}{\mathopen {}\left [\mathbb {T}\right ]\mathclose {}}. The spectrum of a U_{\mathbb {T}}-algebra M is the set of U_{\mathbb {T}}-algebra homomorphisms M\to U_{\mathbb {T}}. (Recall that a U_{\mathbb {T}}-algebra is a model of \mathbb {T} equipped with a homomorphism from U_{\mathbb {T}}.)

Let \mathbb {T} be a horn theory. The generic \mathbb {T}-model U_{\mathbb {T}} is quasicoherent in the following sense: internal to \mathbf {Set}{\mathopen {}\left [\mathbb {T}\right ]\mathclose {}}, for any finitely presentable U_{\mathbb {T}}-algebra A, the canonical U_{\mathbb {T}}-algebra homomorphism A \to U_{\mathbb {T}}^{\operatorname {Spec}{A}} sending a to \lambda f:\operatorname {Spec}{A}. f\,a is an isomorphism, where \operatorname {Spec}{A} is the synthetic spectrum of A.
